item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
All good stories, no matter how fantastical, contain at least a grain of truth. If it helps you or any other readers, I will say that 2739/01 is not based on any single person. He is based largely on two very different people, whose real stories could not be more different. But he is also based on, exists as a monument to, countless others whose stories rhyme with his.

As a work of fiction, this is a condemnation of the 'developed' world's response to substance abuse. It is meant as a condemnation of our failure to help our most vulnerable. We just give them uniforms and rifles and excuses instead.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I am not sure which one hit deeper.
The article, or the line in your comment which change meaning after clicking.
At this moment I don't know if this is fiction, or someones sad reality.

And at this point I really hope its fiction, but deep down I know it might not.

+1. Well deserved.
That's 5 now. I guess somebody around here still likes me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stopping by to say upvoted. An anomalous art installation on the subject of personal loss and struggles works well. PoI notes help give some background context while staying in the format and styling of a SCP article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, everyone loses someone at some point. Death is a part of life, perhaps the only universal part of the human condition. This was written at a time when I was really lost in a lot of ways and facing existential fear and dread over circumstances far beyond my control. It was written to help me deal with how things had turned out for a couple people I really cared about.

I don't know where I'm going with all this, but regardless, I appreciate the sentiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My condolences for your loss, and to everyone who's lost anyone.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Haven't had a proper chance to review the article in question with this crosslink in mind, reverting the edit, sorry for not providing any help when the timer was active.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This is similar in concept to SCP-3429. Both are art exhibits involving anomalous art dedicated to a single purpose; either memorialising somebody or demonizing society.

Editing this:

As the loop continues, objects disturbed by the loop will return to their default position.

To this:

As the loop continues, objects disturbed by the loop will return to their default position.

item.title: Re: Very good!
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for posting almost 1 year later, but I just had to say that I agree. I also disliked it at first when I saw the page filled with blackboxes, but I actually liked it very much when I noticed they had a very objective, clear and consistent purpose.

It was simply protection of personal information and the identities of people involved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very sad. At least the Foundation guard the place, and the tribute that contains. I really like it
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late reply, but your expression of appreciation is enough. If people are upvoting, if this hurts to read (in an emotional sense, not in a readability sense), it means I did my job as an author. And knowing that I got the point across is reward enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As painful as this may have been to read, I can assure you it was twice as painful to write. This hurt. I wasn't in a happy place when I wrote this. Every time I get a review like this, I get the feeling that writing from a place of pain like that was worth it, in its own way. At the same time, though, I don't want to go back to that place again anytime soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I gave some feedback in chat, but it warrants repeating here too: I really like this. Like, really like it. It got difficult to read towards the end (because of the subject matter, not the writing, that was solid), and felt personal, even uncomfortably so — it then takes that discomfort and wields it like a knife, attacking you right in the feels. The fact that it's semi-autobiographical lifts it to another level of painfulness, but it's still painful in all the right ways.

I'll upvote anything that makes me feel emotions besides boredom and maybe anger, and I upvoted this hard.

item.title: Re: Minor rewrite proposal
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the feedback. As I've said upthread and elsewhere, this was really personal for me, and really painful to write.

As it stands, I am a little worried that i spelled everything out too much. Of course, I didn't think this needed an interview. This piece is kind of meta; the artist is, to some extent, a self-insert. One thing she and I would agree on is that this thing should stand on its own without someone there to hold the hand of the person experiencing the art. It's a biography that ends in suicide, not a children's nursery rhyme book.

item.title: Re: Minor rewrite proposal
item.content:encoded:
I might be interested in seeing some more, listed on another page (sort of a 'Link to full catalog of objects found within SCP-2739)

As far as the PoI logs, at least where the idea of PoI has developed, I think the artist doesn't need a full log, while the subject of the art wouldn't really count as a PoI as I understand it, but at least for now, the summary of their life is helpful to have (Perhaps list them as 'the former occupant of SCP-2739,')

I really like how you tell a story without relying on excess narrative. My biggest annoyance since I started browsing the wiki again has been the excessive reliance on spelling things out for me, since much of the (for lack of a better word) fun for me is in putting things together. And all the art in here really works well. A worse author would have burdened it with interviews with the PoI and maybe even one of the artworks displaying some level of intelligence. By just telling us what the person is and what the art shows, we can feel it stronger than if we had a long interview. This is one of my favorite SCPs yet.

item.title: Minor rewrite proposal
item.content:encoded:
I know I do this with my own works a lot, and for that I apologize. However, this post isn't for a meta discussion of my bad habits as a community member; rather, it's for a proposal.

The PoI logs are/were a source of contention for this. So I'm wondering whether it'd be better for the article's readability to remove the logs entirely, or place them in a separate, supplemental page. Additionally, I had some ideas for more art pieces. The article mentions non-anomalous artworks existing within the exhibit. Would there be any interest in seeing some?

Any input on this is appreciated.

item.title: Re: Very good!
item.content:encoded:
The blackboxing is an intentional stylistic choice. Specifically, a lot of the blackboxes (and other censors) conceal names inspired by (or lifted from) reality. As implied by my opening post, this is partially inspired by real people and events. So the censor here serves three purposes:

  1. Limit the possibility of connections being made between the reality and the fiction
  2. Imply that the Foundation is trying to prevent the equivalent from happening in-universe (so, say, an agent stationed near SCP-2739 won't go and murder this dude's parents for child abuse, using his status as a Foundation agent to remain secret)
  3. This stuff happens all the damn time, all over the place. I didn't want to apply an identity to these characters so it might be easier for the readers to apply their own; these people could be from a major city, or a small town. They could be from the desert or the coast or the mountains or anywhere in-between. They could be black, white, hispanic, Middle-Eastern, Native American, or any other conceivable ethnic/race category. They could be gay, straight, bi, closeted (or unacknowledged by the Foundation as) transgender, or fall into a category outside the easily-defined spectrum of gender & sexuality. These people could be anybody. These kinds of struggles don't know any real boundary. I wanted to make a point of letting the reader form an image in their own mind of who these people are.

I hate overuse of the censor when it's arbitrary, but I didn't feel it was arbitrary here.

item.title: Very good!
item.content:encoded:
Very good, well written. +1

My one critique is that there seems to be a lot of unnecessary black boxing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very good. Very touching. A +1 is all I can give, but I feel like I should have given something else more substantial for this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I'm talking like… handcuffs with a syringe.

Think of it as a sculpture utilizing a nontraditional medium. The title of the piece, the associated cognitohazard, it's all the artist trying to say something.

Item is a pair of handcuffs of the same model as those issued to the ██████, ██ Police Department from ████ to ████ and a hypodermic needle within a plexiglass display case.

The two items are together to indicate that they're really the same thing.

When directly observed by a human being, the handcuffs appear unlocked and the needle appears to be capped and sealed in a sterilized pouch, but when recorded with digital and film photography, the handcuffs appear locked and the needle appears to be broken.

Human sight can be fallible, can be subjective. A camera, unless tampered with, cannot. So the handcuffs only look unlocked, the needle only looks clean. They're not.

A plaque is fixed to the display case that reads: "Chains." This is presumably the title of the piece.

The artist is playing with perception again. She's saying that the drugs and the system in place for dealing with addiction are really the same thing and that thing is shackles, holding people like her brother back.

As I said, think of it as a sculpture. Instead of clay and marble, the artist just used extant objects. Instead of angle tricks, she used some sort of magic to make it look different depending on how it's viewed.

Tl;dr: Are We Cool Yet?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes, definitely. However, subjectivity is the basis of most more modern forms of art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, there are plenty of art installations that are that simple. But heck, the true art of that particular piece involves how it is both effective and ineffective, depending on how you view it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: does art really even have an objective meaning?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm talking like… handcuffs with a syringe. Anyway, that doesn't matter. Knowing 203 is the last one is good. I mean, it kind of feels like it sums up what all the others imply, but on the other hand, driving the message home is probably not a bad idea, for those less well-versed in the abstract.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And how is most of this 'art'?

This is what real meaningful art looks like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
how the hell many art pieces are there in this thing? O.o

203. Last one listed here is the last one out of all of 'em.

And how is most of this 'art'?

A shadow play isn't art? A sculpture isn't art?

I doubt they'll ever find her, if she had enough power to make all that stuff, is all I'm sayin'.

As long as she doesn't hurt anyone, the Foundation doesn't really care. And she fell off the map for three years after dropping out of uni. Who's to say she didn't keep studying, perhaps in a certain well-equipped Library?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sounds like the anartist just wanted people to understand what happened to her brother. That's compelling and worth an upvote.

That said, shit, how the hell many art pieces are there in this thing? O.o And how is most of this 'art'? I doubt they'll ever find her, if she had enough power to make all that stuff, is all I'm sayin'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be honest, I'm not skilled enough to write something like Tabula Rasa for this concept. /02 is more of a self-insert than anything. She's an author mouthpiece.

Took the better judgment of my reviewers not to turn this into a blatant PSA. I'm glad it seems to be hitting readers the way I intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you decided to write this after all.

Been trying to figure out what you mean by this all day; this project has been chugging forward (slowly) since I pitched the idea to you. I just write slowly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty damn solid and quite moving - I'm tearing up ;0; Easy +1.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Doing as good as ever. Just had some demons I wanted to exorcise onto the page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first time I read this, I figured it was too straightforward. But I think it's that simplicity that makes it tragically beautiful.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was powerful. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you decided to write this after all. It's very powerful and disturbing.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This was dark, somber and quite heartfelt. I really dig what you did here.

+1 Hope you doing okay, my dude.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thank you to everyone who reviewed.

This is a work of fiction. Resemblance with real persons and events is not intentional.
This is not a work of fiction. Resemblance with real persons and events is intentional. I'm sorry.
